texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of carbon dioxide from mixed streams of hydrogen and nitrogen is critical to many industries such as hydrogen production, ammonia production, fuel cell technology, and flue gas purification. Fuel cell technology has the advantages of increased efficient fuel consumption and decreased environmental pollution. Proton-exchange membranes (PEMs) are being developed for vehicles due to their advantages of rapid start, compactness, and low projected cost [1,2]. Hydrogen concentrations in the feedstream of the fuel cell may range from 30 to 75% and depend on the primary fuel and its processing [3]. Higher purity hydrogen feeds result in increased PEM fuel cells efficiencies. Carbon dioxide in the feedstream acts as a diluent and can produce carbon monoxide via the reverse water-gas shift reaction. Carbon monoxide acts as a poison to the platinum catalyst at low concentrations (10-100 ppm [4]). CO2-selective membranes could be used to remove CO2 from fuel cell feedstreams, or to purify the retentate stream for recycle. PEM fuel cells reject 20-25% of entering hydrogen [5,6].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of carbon dioxide from hydrogen and nitrogen at high temperatures would be valuable to fuel cell, flue gas purification, and ammonia processes. A feed gas mixture of carbon dioxide, hydrogen and nitrogen (10% CO2, 10% H2, and 80% N2) was used to evaluate water-swollen chitosan as a facilitated transport membrane for these applications. The amino group of the chitosan repeating unit could be the fixed carrier that facilitates carbon dioxide transport in the presence of water.
texts.text: The separation of carbon dioxide from hydrogen and nitrogen at high temperatures would be valuable to fuel cell, flue gas purification, and ammonia processes. A feed gas mixture of carbon dioxide, hydrogen and nitrogen (10% CO2, 10% H2, and 80% N2) was used to evaluate water-swollen chitosan as a facilitated transport membrane for these applications. The amino group of the chitosan repeating unit could be the fixed carrier that facilitates carbon dioxide transport in the presence of water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide transport properties of a water-swollen chitosan membrane were measured over temperature and pressure ranges of 20-150 ◦ C and 1.5-5 atm, respectively. The average levels of water (total, free and bound) in the membranes were measured and correlated with carbon dioxide transport properties. At a feedstream pressure of 1.5 atm, all free water was lost from the membrane at temperatures above 110 ◦ C and only bound water could contribute to facilitated transport. This mechanism change resulted in maxima in the CO2 permeability (482 barrers), the CO2/H2 selectivity (43.4), and the CO2/N2 selectivity (250) at T =110 ◦ C. Each type of water appeared to have a different efficiency in contributing to the facilitated transport of carbon dioxide through chitosan membranes.

texts.orig: Facilitated transport membranes, in which different carriers complex with carbon dioxide to increase its permeability, have been developed. Mobile carriers include: chlorides [7], carbonates/bicarbonates [7-9], glycine [7,9-11], carbonates/glycine [10], ethylenediamine [7,10,12-17], glycine-ethylenediamine [10], diethanolamine [18], polyamidoamine dendrimer [19-21], anions of ethylmethylimidazolium-bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl) amide, ethylmethylimidazolium trifluoromethanesulfone, ethylmethylimidazolium dicyanamide, and trihexyltetradecylphosphonium chloride bis(fluoromethanesulfonyl) amide [22], and 1-butyl-3-methyl imidazolium-bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl) amide [23]. Others researchers have used membranes with fixed carrier amino groups to separate CO2 from N2 such as polyethyleneimine/polyvinylbutyral [24], poly(2-( N , N -dimethyl)aminoethyl methacrylateco -acrylonitrile) and poly (2-( N , N -dimethyl)aminoethyl methacrylateco -2-ethylhexyl methacrylate) [25,26], copolyimides modified by various amines[27], poly(2-( N , N -dimethyl)aminoethyl methacrylate) [28], poly(vinylbenzyltrimethyl ammonium fluoride) [29], and polyethyleneimine/poly(vinyl alcohol) [28].

Quinn and Laciak [29] have prepared poly(vinylbenzyltrimethyl ammonium fluoride) membranes to separate CO2 from H2 by fixed carrier transport. The addition of cesium fluoride to poly(vinylbenzyltrimethyl ammoniumfluoride) membranes increased the CO2 permeability and the CO2/H2 selectivity by ∼ 2.5-fold and ∼ 1.5-fold, respectively [32]. Multilayer membranes, with both poly(diallyldimethylammonium fluoride) and poly(vinylbenzyltrimethyl ammonium fluoride) layers, have been developed to reduce defects[33]. The addition of an extra layer has increased CO2/H2 selectivities and CO2 permeances by factors of 2-18 and 1.3-2.3, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ho[34] has used the hydrophilic polymer, poly(vinyl alcohol), with amino-containing acids (glycine, pipecolinic acid, and aminoisobutyric acid) plus monovalent cations (lithium, potassium, and ethylenediamine) to separate CO2 from H2-rich streams by facilitated transport. Different amines, i.e., ethanolamine and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol, have also been tried, but these systems did not exhibit higher CO2 permeability or higher CO2/H2 selectivity [35]. Ho [36] has introduced polyethylenimine to a blend of poly(vinyl alcohol) and glycine-lithium to separate CO2 from H2 at a temperature range of 21-80 ◦ C, improving CO2 transport properties. Tee et al. [37] enhanced CO2 transport properties through cross-linked poly(vinyl alcohol) blended with polyethyleneimine and dimethylglycine for temperatures up to 90 ◦ C. Zou and Ho [38] blended 2-aminoisobutyric acid, poly(allylamine) and cross-linked poly(vinyl alcohol) and have reported high CO2 permeabilities and selectivities at temperatures higher than 100 ◦ C. They show that as the temperature increases, CO2 transport properties decrease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chitosan is a deacetylated derivative from chitin, an abundant natural polymer. Swollen chitosan membranes have been used for the separation of CO2/N2. Ito et al. [40] separated a 50%:50% CO2 /N2 mixture through a chitosan membrane that has been swollen by humidifying the feed gas. Their CO2 permeabilities were 70-100 barrers with selectivities of 70-100 at a feed pressure of 3.5 atm over a temperature range of 22-60 ◦ C. Bae et al. [41] studied wet chitosan membranes for the permeation of pure CO2 and N2 at 20 ◦ C and 30 ◦ C for a feed pressure range between 5 and 20atm, but no differential pressure. They have achieved CO2 permeabilities under 1 barrer with selectivities ranging from 28 to 37. These studies demonstrate that maintaining water in chitosan membrane gives higher carbon dioxide permeabilities and selectivities, even at higher temperatures. Neither study measured water levels in their membranes at use conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Interactions of carbon dioxide with the amino and hydroxyl groups of chitosan in the presence of water are central to the membrane's facilitated transport mechanisms. Related work on the chemistry of carbon dioxide uptake into amine solutions has some relevance here. The absorption of carbon dioxide into aqueous alkanolamine solutions has been well studied [42-45] as such systems are used commercially to remove CO2 from natural gas and refinery gas streams. The presence of water in these solutions is known to change the anions and cations produced during carbon dioxide sorption, the molecular conformations of the alkanolamines, and the intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonding [43,44]. Several researchers have developed kinetic models for these complex mechanisms [42,45]. Therefore, mea- surement of water content in chitosan membranes is critical to interpreting facilitated transport mechanisms for carbon dioxide.

texts.orig: where the three bold dots represent an ionic bond. Carbamic acid (represented by R-NH(CO)-OH in this reaction sequence) can be generated by bubbling carbon dioxide through solutions of primary amines in polar aprotic solvents, such as DMSO and DMF [47]. Carbamic acids in these solvents appear to be stable, as established by NMRmeasurements[48].DuringCO2 sorption into alcohol amines, nearby hydroxyl groups can stabilize the hydrogen bonding with thegas,resultinginpreferredproductconformationsthathavehigh stability [43,44]. The formation of a carbamate salt is essentially an acid-base equilibrium between carbamic acid and a second amine. As suggested by the reaction sequence, the formation of carbamic acids and carbamate salts is reversible. The rate of the reverse reaction of carbamate salts back to their corresponding acids increases with temperature, so removing carbon dioxide and heating the system can reform the amines [48].

texts.orig: The glucopyranose repeating unit of chitosan has one primary amino group in each of its repeating units. There are two hydroxyl groups in each repeating unit, one on the ring and one in the hydroxylmethyl group, which could help stabilize reaction products. Therefore, chitosan's chemical structure has some unique attributes for enhancing reactions with carbon dioxide.

texts.text: 2.2. States of water in chitosan

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are several prior studies that describe the states of water in hydrogels, three-dimensional polymer networks that swell in aqueous solutions, and specifically, chitosan. Qu et al. [49] propose three types of water, bound water that is hydrogen-bonded to hydrophilic groups, freezing bound water that forms secondary andtertiary 'shells' or clusters around bound water, and free water that is a separate phase in the polymer and has no freezing point depression.TheirDSCdataofwaterfreezingrangesinaswollenchitosan show that no water freezing point is observed for water levels up to 24-28wt%, suggesting that all of this water is bound to the polymer matrix. Fig. 1 illustrates two different hydrogen-bonding configurations between water and the primary amine group on the chitosan repeating unit.

texts.orig: Lee et al. [50] reported the equilibrium moisture content of chitosan at 25 ◦ C as 59.5 wt% with 31.6 wt% free water and 27.9 wt% boundwater. The equilibrium moisture content was determined by comparing the weights of initial and swollen samples. DSC scans of water-saturated chitosan samples showed two distinct peaks, one associated with free water and the other associated with bound water. The amounts of each type were computed by assuming that theheatoffusionofpurewaterappliedtobothtypes,anddetermining the peak areas. Thermogravimetric analysis of swollen chitosan samples also showed two water loss stages, one just above 100 ◦ C and one near 150 ◦ C.

texts.orig: In this paper, swollen chitosan membranes were used to separate CO2 from CO2/H2/N2 mixtures for the first time. Low levels of water in chitosan should activate the facilitated transport of carbon dioxide, while the hydrogen and nitrogen should be transported by solution-diffusion mechanisms. Measurements of total water, bound water and free water were linked to the carbon dioxide permeabilities, carbon dioxide fluxes, and the CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 separation factors. These effects were investigated at three different temperatures (20, 110 and 150 ◦ C) over the feed pressure range of 1.5-5 atm.

texts.orig: 3.2. Membrane synthesis
texts.text: 3.2. Membrane synthesis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Purified chitosan was dissolved in 1 vol.% acetic acid to make a 1 wt% casting solution. Films were cast onto the Tetratex using a casting knife. The cast membrane was first dried at room temperature for 72h. The membrane was placed in a temperature programmed vacuum oven. The drying temperature was ramped at 1 ◦ C/min from 22 to 120 ◦ C, held for 6 h, ramped at 1 ◦ C/min to 150 ◦ C, and held for 6 h. Finally, the oven was turned off and the chitosan membrane was cooled overnight to room temperature. The average final thickness of membrane, 65 𝛍 m, was measured by an electronic micrometer. The effective membrane area was calculated to be 49 cm 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas permeation apparatus and method used were similar to those described by Tee et al. [37]. A simplified schematic of the gas permeation unit is shown in Fig. 2. The process operating conditions and control units are listed in Table A (Appendix A). The feed gas, sweep gas, and water were supplied to the humidifiers. The humidified feed gas and sweep gas flow countercurrently across the membrane. The gases exited the oven and they were dehumidified via water knockouts. Pulse flows from the retentate and the permeate streams were sent to the GC system for analysis. Humidities were calculated from material balances of the gas and liquid streams around the apparatus, and confirmed by weighing the water caught via the water knockouts.

texts.orig: 3.5. Water analysis
texts.text: 3.5. Water analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior work with thermogravimetric analysis of swollen chitosan demonstrated that all free water is lost from the polymer at temperatures greater than 100 ◦ C, and all bound water was lost at temperatures above 150 ◦ C. After permeation studies, membrane samples were immediately weighed to determine the total weight of the swollen polymer (polymer + bound water + free water). Since the membrane temperature dropped rapidly when it was removed from the apparatus, water loss was thought to be low during the weighing process. The samples were then dried to constant weight at 100 ◦ C (polymer+bound water). Drying the membrane samples to constant weight at 150 ◦ C gave the polymer weight. Drying to constant weight at either temperature required several hours. These three weights were used to determine the total wt% water at the particular operating condition, and the fractions of bound and free water of this total.

texts.text: 4.1.1. Effect of sweep's relative humidity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sweep gas relative humidity was varied from 1.5 to 20.1% while the feed gas was kept at a constant relative humidity of 14.6%. Fig. 3 shows the effect of varying the sweep gas relative humidity on the bound water and the CO2 permeability. Higher sweep gas relative humidity increases the water activity at the membrane's surface, therefore increasing water in the membrane. Bound water wouldbeimmediatelyavailabletofacilitatethevariousreactionsof CO2 as it hops from one amine site to another. Hopping mechanisms are usually invoked to describe the selective transport of penetrants in fixed carrier membranes [52]. The sweep gas volumetric flowrate is increased significantly by the water vapor, reducing its CO2 concentration and increasing the driving force for CO2 transport. All the above factors enhance the kinetic and dynamic aspects of the fixed facilitated transport of CO2 , therefore leading to higher CO2 permeabilities.

Table 1 shows that the CO2/H2 and CO2/N2 selectivities increase from 13 to 29 and 90 to 194, respectively, as the sweep's relative humidity increase from 1.5 to 14.5%. While the permeation of CO2 is controlled by facilitated transport, H2 and N2 permeate via the solution-diffusion mechanism. Clearly, the facilitated transport mechanism controls the permeability and selectivity for CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure studies reveal carrier saturation which limits gas transport in facilitated transport membranes. The feed pressure was varied from 1.5 to 5 atm at the following temperatures, 150, 110, and 20 ◦ C, while the sweep pressure was maintained at atmospheric pressure. In this series of experiments, the relative humidities of the feed and sweep gases were manipulated to maintain an average water content in the chitosan membrane of 8.3wt%. On a molar basis, this total water wt% corresponds to 0.8 mol of water per chitosan repeating unit. This level of total water in the membrane could be accomplished over the whole temperature range. Table 2 shows the relative humidities of the feed and sweep gas streams needed to achieve this water level at a feed pressure of 1.5 atm. The pairings of relative humidities were determined by trial and error, and were used for experiments at higher feed gas pressures.

texts.orig: The type of water in the membrane also affects carbon dioxide transport. Table 3 shows the different forms of water (bound and free) and their percentages for 150, 110, and 20 ◦ C at the six feed pressures. At 150 ◦ C, CO2 facilitated transport initiated by bound water is dominant as no free water exists in the membrane. At 110 ◦ C, the total water content drops some as pressure is increased. However, the fraction of bound water decreases dramatically, from 89% at 1 atm feed pressure to 31% at 5 atm feed pressure. The CO2 permeability shows a change in slope between 3 and 4 atm. At low pressures, the fraction of bound water is high, and its effects on the complexation reactions dominated the carbon dioxide transport. At high pressures, the fraction of free water is high, and it controls the facilitated transport process. At 20 ◦ C, the bound water content is only 21% of the total water at a feed pressure of 1.5 atm. Bound water decreases to 2% at a feed pressure of 5atm. Facilitated transport of CO2 via free water must be the key mechanism at this temperature. Therefore, the form of water is responsible for the different decreasing behaviors of CO2 permeabilities at different temperatures. Potential changes in the dominant mechanism may exist in other facilitated transport membranes, such as, poly(vinyl alcohol)/dimethylglycine-lithium [37] and poly[(2N , N -dimethyl) aminoethyl methacrylate] [57]. However, these studies did not report the fractions of bound or free water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Temperature studies should demonstrate differences in the transport mechanisms of CO2. The temperature was varied from 20 to 150 ◦ C at feed pressures of 1.5 and 5 atm, while the sweep pressure was maintained at atmospheric pressure. Table 4 shows the percentages of bound water and free water in the chitosan membrane for each pressure over the temperature range, 20-150 ◦ C. With the feed stream at 1.5 atm pressure, there is a transition in the forms of water in the chitosan membrane at 110 ◦ C. Below 110 ◦ C, water exists in both forms of water (bound and free). Above 110 ◦ C, only bound water exists due to the depletion of free water. As the feed pressure is increased to 5atm, both bound and free water exists throughout the temperature range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 11 shows the plot of CO2 permeability vs. the inverse of absolute temperature for the above operating conditions The peak is caused by the loss of free water at 110 ◦ C, which can contribute both to facilitated transport and to either solution-diffusion or water transport. The existence of a peak is explained by the bound and free water data (Table 4). However, the 'sharpness' of the peak depends on which transport mechanisms are occurring on each side of the maximum. At temperatures greater than 110 ◦ C, only facilitated transport via bound water can occur, while several transport mechanisms are possible below this temperature.

texts.orig: Table 6 compares our results with previous results for chitosan membranes and other fixed carrier membranes for the separation of CO2/H2 and CO2/N2. The prominence of this work lay in that there are no literature accounts of the separation of CO2 from a ternary mixture of CO2/H2/N2 by fixed carrier membranes at temperatures greater than 80 ◦ C. The humidification of the feed and sweep gas retained water in chitosan membrane, greatly enhancing its CO2 transport properties. The best results for the swollen chitosan membrane, in this work, are achieved at 110 ◦ C and 1.5 atm . The CO2 permeability through our swollen chitosan membrane, at 110 ◦ C, exceeds all other fixed carrier membranes except for polyethyleneimine/poly(vinyl alcohol). On the other hand, the selectivities of our membrane are higher. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Swollen chitosan membranes acted as fixed facilitated transport membranes and separated CO2 from the ternary mixture of CO2/H2/N2 at a temperature range of 20-150 ◦ C and feed pressure range of 1.5-5 atm. The average levels of total water, free water and bound water were measured and linked to the carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes, and separation factors with respect to hydrogen and nitrogen.
texts.text: Swollen chitosan membranes acted as fixed facilitated transport membranes and separated CO2 from the ternary mixture of CO2/H2/N2 at a temperature range of 20-150 ◦ C and feed pressure range of 1.5-5 atm. The average levels of total water, free water and bound water were measured and linked to the carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes, and separation factors with respect to hydrogen and nitrogen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Free water in the membrane decreased with temperature, and increased with feedstream pressure. When operating conditions excluded free water from the membrane ( T =150 ◦ C), the carbon dioxide permeabilities and selectivities correlated directly with bound water levels. As feed pressure increased, carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes and selectivities decreased, but the controlling mechanisms depended on operating conditions. At the lowest feedstream pressure (1.5atm), all free water was lost from the membrane at temperatures above 110 ◦ C. This resulted in maxima for carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes and selectivities.
texts.text: Free water in the membrane decreased with temperature, and increased with feedstream pressure. When operating conditions excluded free water from the membrane ( T =150 ◦ C), the carbon dioxide permeabilities and selectivities correlated directly with bound water levels. As feed pressure increased, carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes and selectivities decreased, but the controlling mechanisms depended on operating conditions. At the lowest feedstream pressure (1.5atm), all free water was lost from the membrane at temperatures above 110 ◦ C. This resulted in maxima for carbon dioxide permeabilities, fluxes and selectivities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Total water levels, and the fraction of bound and free water, played a critical role in CO2 transport in chitosan membranes. Each type of water appears to have a different efficacy on the facilitated transport mechanism, possibly related to their ability to catalyze complexationreactionsbetweentheCO2 andthevariousfunctional groups on the chitosan repeating unit. Therefore, measurement of water and its states in membranes for which it contributes to the facilitated transport mechanisms should aid fundamental understanding of these systems.
